# Questions in Livestream 155

Live Stream #155: The Tyranny of Hypocrisy

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=560" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:20</a> | Did the characters of the Apocrypha actually exist? | He says some did and some did not, so each figure has to be evaluated case by case. | He distinguishes historical existence from the claims made by apocryphal books. Famous figures may have secondary evidence, Enoch may have existed without writing 1 Enoch, and Judith is treated as a literary figure rather than a historical person. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=656" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:56</a> | Why is biblical ritual material so repetitive instead of more philosophical? | He says passages like Numbers 29 reflect Bronze Age ritual-calendar genre rather than later philosophical writing. | He argues that expecting philosophical prose from Bronze Age or Iron Age writers imports a later genre into the text. Numbers 29 repeats ritual details because it is organizing offerings by festival days and quantities, not trying to state theology in abstract terms. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=945" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:45</a> | Why was the exodus pharaoh not subjected to damnatio memoriae? | He says not every pharaoh received that treatment, and Ramesses II's long reign and succession context gave no obvious reason for it. | He explains that damnatio memoriae was exceptional, usually aimed at heretics, usurpers, or rulers with bad successor relationships. If Ramesses II was the exodus pharaoh, his long reign, many successors, and late-life timing would not have created the kind of succession grievance that normally prompted erasure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=1134" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:54</a> | Did Akhenaten's damnatio memoriae begin under Tutankhamun or later? | He says the exact starting point is uncertain and may belong more to the later cleanup under his successors. | He says the start of Akhenaten's erasure is not clear. Tutankhamun, Smenkhkare, and Ay still belonged to the Amarna system, so he suspects the major cleanup may fit better with Horemheb, an outsider who opposed the corruption of that period. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=1231" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:31</a> | What does Hagar's name mean in Egyptian? | He says the name may preserve an Egyptian form that was slightly adjusted in Israelite transmission, and probably carried a sense like poor, hungry, or disadvantaged. | He proposes that Hagar reflects an Egyptian name reshaped in Israelite transmission, with the q and g sounds plausibly interchanging and a feminine ending likely dropped. He connects the Egyptian sense to hunger, poverty, or low status, fitting a disadvantaged background. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=1399" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:19</a> | In Judges 1, were places like Beth-shean and Megiddo still Egyptian garrison towns rather than simply Canaanite towns? | He says they were Egyptian-controlled garrison centers, but the biblical wording can still function as a geographic label within Canaan. | He says Beth-shean and similar sites were Egyptian garrison towns and took a long time to fall under Israelite control because they had armed chariot garrisons. Calling them Canaanite is best read geographically, since these were mixed towns located in traditional Canaanite territory. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=1553" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:53</a> | Why does God tell Balaam to go and then become angry when he goes? | He says Balaam keeps pressing for permission, receives a constrained allowance, and then goes in a disobedient spirit anyway. | He walks through Numbers 22 as an escalating exchange: God first tells Balaam not to go, then Balaam keeps entertaining Balak's richer delegation, and God permits only the specific words he commands. The anger comes because Balaam goes while still pursuing the wrong intent. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=2035" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:55</a> | What are his thoughts on Alex O'Connor, Cosmic Skeptic? | He says he has watched little of O'Connor, finds him generally reasonable but not especially interesting or insightful. | He says he has only seen a few snippets and places O'Connor among less obnoxious atheist apologists, but not among the particularly insightful or philosophically interesting ones. He finds some of the arguments close to new-atheist lines and sometimes straw-manning. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=2163" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:03</a> | What is his position on the philosophy of language? | He says he has no developed position because his philosophy work focused elsewhere. | He says he has no position because he has not done the relevant reading. During his philosophy degree, his interests were philosophy of science and philosophy of religion, not philosophy of language. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=2196" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:36</a> | Why is he a Christian? | He answers "grace," then explains that hardship and crises of faith repeatedly drove him back to God and evidence. | He gives a personal answer rooted in grace rather than a neat argument. He describes a poor church experience, repeated crises over evil and biblical difficulties, and times when he felt God met him with the evidence and help he needed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=2688" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:48</a> | Why do people pray? | He says prayer aligns a person's mind with God's thoughts and helps Christians endure hardship. | He says prayer is not mainly about avoiding hardship, since hardship still comes. Its purpose is to bring one's thoughts into alignment with God's thoughts and to endure trials with God rather than facing them alone. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=2849" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:29</a> | What are his thoughts on the transcendental argument for God's existence? | He says he is not familiar enough with the argument to comment. | He explicitly declines to answer because he is not an apologist and does not know the transcendental argument well enough. He does not try to evaluate it from partial familiarity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=2898" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:18</a> | What is the problem with atheism? | He says atheism poorly accounts for phenomenology and has pragmatic problems, including lack of theistic support structures and forgiveness. | He argues that atheism leaves phenomenological questions, such as substance dualism, poorly accounted for while often leaving naturalism vague. He also sees pragmatic weaknesses because atheism lacks the moral framework, divine relationship, support, and forgiveness Christianity provides. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=3051" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:51</a> | Which logical fallacy is most interesting to him? | He says one related to the dictionary fallacy interests him, but he will not give away the upcoming video. | He avoids naming the fallacy because it would spoil an upcoming video. He only says it is related to the dictionary fallacy, not identical to it, and that he finds it especially interesting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=3121" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:01</a> | What are his thoughts on Joshua Bowen and Kip Davis's MythVision response to Gavin Ortlund's slavery video? | He says he saw pieces of it and thought their response was juvenile, insulting, and contextually unfair. | He says he saw parts of their response and Ortlund's reply, and objects to their tone, insults, and alleged straw-manning. He especially criticizes taking Ortlund's comments out of context and treating remarks aimed at another channel as though they were personal attacks on them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=3643" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:43</a> | Why did Tutankhamun's widow ask the Hittites for a prince to marry? | He says her local options were bad and the request makes sense as a desperate political move. | He says her likely Egyptian options were Ay, an elderly and conniving vizier, or Horemheb, a non-royal military man. Since neither matched what she wanted politically or personally, asking Suppiluliuma for a royal Hittite prince was an attempt to find a third option. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=3799" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:03:19</a> | Would he make a video on Elephantine and what it says about Israelite religion? | He says he might someday, but Elephantine mainly shows a community that quickly syncretized after fleeing to Egypt. | He says Elephantine does not reveal a huge amount about Israelite religion, though it may be worth a future video. He describes the community as Judeans who fled after Gedaliah's assassination, built a temple, spoke mostly Aramaic, and quickly mixed Yahweh worship with other deities. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=3926" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:26</a> | Is learning Japanese by reading the Bible in Japanese and English a good plan? | He says it is not a great method for learning modern Japanese because Bible translations often use archaic language. | He says Bible translations often preserve older, formal, or archaic language, including Japanese phrasing he compares to language from around the 1930s. That may help someone read the Bible in Japanese, but it is a poor path for learning modern colloquial Japanese. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=4081" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:01</a> | Are there extant execration texts related to Israel or Jerusalem? | He says some execration texts name places in Israel, including Jerusalem, mostly dating to the Middle Kingdom, but not Israel as a whole. | He explains execration texts as curses written on figurines or jars and smashed as ritual magic, often preserving personal or place names. He says they are useful for geography but scattered in publication, and that Jerusalem and other places appear while Israel as a national entity does not. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=4295" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:35</a> | Does Genesis 3:17 conflict with an evolutionary view of earth's history? | He says it neither supports nor clearly conflicts with an evolutionary view, because the passage is about human toil and the cursed ground. | He reads Genesis 3:17 as saying Adam will now have to work the cursed ground in toil. He does not see that as prima facie conflicting with evolutionary history, but he also says the verse does not support evolution either. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=4468" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:28</a> | What is progressive creationism? | He describes it as a view with long or indeterminate creation ages in which God creates progressively throughout those ages. | He contrasts it with young-earth creationism's six literal days and old-earth creationism's long ages with creative bursts at the start. Progressive creationism, as he defines it, has long or indeterminate ages in which God continues creating throughout each age. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=4599" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:39</a> | How do henotheism, monolatry, and monotheism differ? | He says henotheism is a kind of monolatry, while monotheism denies the existence of other gods. | He defines monolatry as worship or allegiance directed to one deity, a category that can include monotheism or even pantheism. Henotheism is a narrower form in which many gods are acknowledged but allegiance is owed to one; monotheism denies that the other gods exist. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=4718" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:38</a> | What is the difference between monolatry and monarchial monotheism? | He says monolatry is a practice of worshiping one God, while monarchial monotheism is a theology with one supreme God over lesser spiritual beings. | He distinguishes worship practice from divine ontology. Monolatry is giving worship to one God, while monarchial monotheism says one supreme God rules over angels, spirits, or lesser heavenly beings rather than leaving only God and humanity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=4908" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:48</a> | What were the alcohol contents of Egyptian wine and beer? | He says ordinary Egyptian beer was weak, often about 1-3% ABV, while wine could be around 12% and sometimes higher if fortified with dates. | He says Egyptian beer was usually weak unless fortified with dates, while wine commonly reached about 12 percent. Adding dates could raise the sugar content and theoretically produce stronger beer or wine, though he does not present that as the normal case. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=5005" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:25</a> | Were Medjay really always drunk? | He says the texts often characterize Medjay that way, probably because guard duty in hot, boring outposts made drinking common. | He connects the stereotype to Middle Kingdom prophetic texts and to the nature of Medjay guard work. In hot, dull outposts or Sinai forts, sitting around drinking beer or wine could become common, even though their formal role was maintaining order. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=5136" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:36</a> | Could Amarel come from a later rendition of Hammurabi's name? | He says it is possible and can see the proposed transliteration happening. | He says the proposal is possible, especially if the form is closer to Amari rather than straight Hammurabi. He notes that the rounded h sound could be transliterated in a way that makes the suggested development plausible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=5198" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:38</a> | What does 2 Corinthians 3:17-18 mean by "the Lord is the Spirit"? | He connects it to Moses' veil and says Paul is presenting Yahweh as spirit, with believers now seeing and living in that revealed glory. | He reads the passage through Moses' veil, where Moses covered the reflected glory after coming down from the mountain. Paul, in his explanation, connects turning to the Lord with the veil being removed and believers beholding and being transformed by the Lord's glory through the Spirit. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=5402" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:02</a> | Why are miracles distinguished from healings in 1 Corinthians 12:28? | He says not all miracles are healings and not all healings are miracles, since healing can include Spirit-guided care rather than only supernatural events. | He says Paul's list separates gifts because the categories overlap without being identical. Some miracles are not healings, and some healings may involve practical wisdom and care, as with early Christians tending the sick during plagues, rather than only spectacular supernatural events. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=5639" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:59</a> | What is the difference between Aten and Atum? | He says Aten is the sun disk associated with Akhenaten's pantheistic worship, while Atum is an older Heliopolitan creation deity. | He says the two names are spelled differently in Egyptian and refer to completely different gods. Aten is the sun disk and the focus of Akhenaten's pantheistic Atenism, while Atum is an Old Kingdom creation deity from Heliopolis, often paired with Re. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=5754" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:54</a> | What do vampire cults do or believe? | He says they generally believe in living vampires, magic, exclusive cult membership, pessimism toward humanity, moral codes, and psychic vampirism. | He describes living vampires as people believed to feed on human energy rather than walking corpses, though groups disagree on the mechanism. He says common features include occult practice, exclusivity, gloomy views of humanity, internal moral codes, and ideas of psychic or energetic feeding. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=6114" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:54</a> | Is the Great Sphinx basically a cherub, and what roles did cherubs and seraphs have? | He says sphinxes symbolize royal power, while cherubim and seraphim protect sacred space. | He rejects identifying the Great Sphinx as a cherub because Egyptian sphinxes symbolize royal power and authority. Cherubim are protective beings associated with sacred space, and seraphim are fiery cobra-like guardians that keep impurity away. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=6267" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:44:27</a> | If Moses could have written an alphabetic script, which one would it have been? | He says it was probably not Phoenician, but closer to early alphabetic inscriptions known from Egypt and Sinai. | He says Phoenician is unlikely, but early alphabetic scripts from Egypt and Sinai are plausible. He points to inscriptions extending from the Middle Kingdom into Ramesside times and says they are graphically related to Phoenician without being the same script. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=6353" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:53</a> | What heretical things did Ron Wyatt say, and where can they be seen? | He says Wyatt's claims about Jesus' blood and chromosome count deny full humanity, and Wyatt also taught a form of works-based legalism. | He focuses on two claims as sufficient examples: Wyatt's alleged testing of Jesus' blood with only half the normal human chromosome count, which would deny Christ's full humanity, and legalistic teaching he regards as works-based. He points listeners to recordings he reviewed about Wyatt's Ark of the Covenant claims. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=6914" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:14</a> | Were Egyptian soldiers promoted based on equipment rather than experience or achievement? | He says equipment determined eligibility for military classes, with charioteers able to rise higher, but equipment was not the whole story. | He says equipment placed soldiers in classes: archers were lower because their gear was cheap, infantry higher, and charioteers eligible for officer status. Still, equipment created ceilings and eligibility rather than replacing all experience, merit, or achievement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=7104" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:24</a> | Were synagogues understood as Jewish houses of worship before the destruction of the Second Temple? | He says yes, synagogues were local houses of worship, prayer, and study, though not places for temple sacrifice. | He traces the synagogue movement to the Babylonian exile, when deported Judeans lacked access to the temple but still wanted faithful communal practice. Synagogues became meeting places for worship, prayer, and study, while sacrifice remained tied to the temple. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=7255" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:55</a> | How do passages about Satan as god of this age or prince of the power of the air relate to demonic squatters? | He says those titles emphasize temporary, noisy, defeated power rather than true divine authority. | He reads phrases like god of the air or ruler of this age as almost insulting, emphasizing vapor, noise, and ephemeral authority rather than real sovereignty. The language fits his demonic-squatter framing because Satan has disruptive but limited and temporary power. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=7620" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:07:00</a> | What can he say about Tell el-Borg and his contribution to research on Bronze Age arrows and javelins? | He says Tell el-Borg was a Road of Horus fortress, and his work analyzed arrowheads and javelin heads found there. | He identifies Tell el-Borg as a small Road of Horus fortress north of Sinai, probably the Lion's Den on the Karnak route map. His research contribution was analyzing the bronze and some iron arrowheads and javelin heads recovered there. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=7994" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:14</a> | Are there extra-Israelite accounts of the conquest? | He says there are not direct conquest accounts comparable to Joshua, but there is relevant external and archaeological context. | He says no direct extra-Israelite conquest account is known and that this is not surprising, since enemies would be unlikely to record Israelite victories. He allows that a future stela might appear, but expects it would more likely record an Israelite defeat. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=8075" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:14:35</a> | What are his thoughts on the Netflix series Testament: The Story of Moses? | He says he has not watched it and does not have Netflix, though he has not heard good things. | He declines substantive criticism because he has not seen the series and does not have Netflix. He adds only that he has heard negative reactions, including comments about details such as the Ten Commandments being shown incorrectly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=8233" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:13</a> | What are his thoughts on Christians using Ecclesiastes 10:2 as affirmation for a U.S. political party? | He says that reads modern political left and right into the text and is bad exegesis. | He says Ecclesiastes 10:2 is not about modern political right and left, and using it that way imports an anachronistic meaning into the text. He calls that eisegesis and refuses to use the verse to endorse either political side. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=8374" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:34</a> | Has he thought of doing academic lectures online? | He says he already has a YouTube channel and usually reserves formal academic lectures for conferences or publishable work. | He says YouTube already functions as his online lecture outlet, especially the Monday videos. Formal academic lectures take significant preparation, so he usually does them for conferences or, if he writes formal papers, possibly as separate readings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=8489" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:29</a> | What are his thoughts on Heiser saying Genesis 1-11 may have been written during the Babylonian exile, and does that support the documentary hypothesis? | He prefers a preexilic supplementary view and says he does not give credence to the documentary hypothesis. | He says Heiser accepted the documentary hypothesis but he does not, preferring a more Continental supplementary model. He does not object strongly to exilic-dating proposals, but thinks Genesis is better explained as an amalgamation of older material rather than primarily Babylonian-exilic composition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=8723" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:23</a> | How is Egypt's Exodus-era population measured, and what is the margin of error or highest estimate? | He says historical demographics is not his field, but estimates use excavated settlements and food capacity, with perhaps 2-3 million before the Roman period. | He says estimates come from excavated towns and known settlement sizes, such as Lahun, Thebes, Pi-Ramesses, and Avaris, plus food-production capacity. He stresses the uncertainty and says Egypt likely did not exceed about two to three million people before the Roman period. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=8999" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:59</a> | What are his thoughts on Christians becoming politicians? | He says it can be preferable or not depending on ethics, calling, and circumstances rather than a simple rule. | He says Christians may exercise civic duty through politics just as they might through military service, provided they represent Christ well. He does not treat political office as inherently preferable or wrong, but says the ethics depend on behavior and circumstances. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=9106" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:46</a> | What books would he recommend for studying Daniel's history or hermeneutics? | He recommends older notes on Daniel by Wiseman, Mitchell, Martin, and Kitchen, plus commentaries such as Goldingay and Collins with caution. | He recommends the older Tyndale volume Notes on Some Problems in the Book of Daniel by Wiseman, Mitchell, Martin, and Kitchen as interesting despite its age. He also mentions Daniel commentaries such as Goldingay and Collins, while noting caution and not claiming deep recent engagement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=9282" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:42</a> | Does he read hieroglyphs, and how hard is it? | He says yes, and that it is a language to learn: harder than Hebrew, but easier than Akkadian for him. | He says he reads hieroglyphs, but the difficulty is simply that it is another language with its own learning curve. For him it is more difficult than Hebrew and less difficult than Akkadian, though that comparison may not help every learner. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=9334" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:34</a> | Did Jethro advise Moses before or after the giving of the Ten Commandments? | He says it was probably after the giving of the law, and Exodus arranges some material didactically rather than strictly sequentially. | He agrees the advice probably occurred after the giving of the law because Moses is judging legal matters. He explains that Exodus is not always strictly chronological and can arrange events for didactic or teaching purposes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=9471" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:51</a> | What three Bible-related books would he choose at a bookstore? | He recommends Christopher Wright's Old Testament Ethics, Benjamin Noonan's Non-Semitic Loanwords in the Hebrew Bible, and Context of Scripture. | He frames the answer as books he might choose for general usefulness: Wright on Old Testament ethics, Noonan on non-Semitic loanwords, and Hallo and Younger's Context of Scripture. The last is valued as a broad collection of ancient Near Eastern texts relevant to biblical study. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=9818" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:38</a> | Did Abraham leave Haran after Terah died? | He says Acts 7 reads Genesis 11-12 as Abraham leaving after Terah's death, based on the consecutive-vav sequence. | He says Stephen in Acts 7 is correctly reading Genesis as sequential because of the consecutive-vav construction. He acknowledges the age-math difficulty, then argues the Genesis sequence still presents Abraham's departure as after Terah's death. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=10224" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:50:24</a> | Can he talk more about his struggles with faith and how he got through them? | He says humility, prayer for wisdom, evidence, and willingness to keep learning helped him work through biblical difficulties. | He says it helped to begin by admitting he did not know everything and that alleged biblical inconsistencies were not new discoveries hidden from the church. He describes serious high-school faith crises, study, prayer for wisdom, and learning from evidence as ways he worked through them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=10849" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:49</a> | Does the Ugaritic fragment KTU 1.144 refer to Yahweh as son of El? | He says he would need to study the fragment more closely and cannot give a firm answer from the transliteration. | He says Ugaritic is not one of his stronger areas and he would need to look at the fragment carefully before answering. From the transliteration given, he does not immediately see a clear reference to Yahweh as son of El. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=10946" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:02:26</a> | Is Dan McClellan a Mormon? | He describes McClellan as a nominal Mormon who has not denied Mormonism and sometimes defends it while wanting to change it. | He says McClellan remains at least nominally Mormon, sometimes defending Mormonism against critics, while also seeming to want to reshape it from within. He does not present him as a straightforward traditional Mormon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=11072" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:04:32</a> | Is C. D. worth reading if his theory is rejected? | He says the writer can be useful on Hebrew and Iron Age material, but not on Egypt-related claims. | He says the scholar is strong where his training lies, especially Hebrew and Iron Age Syro-Palestinian archaeology. He warns that the work becomes unreliable when it ventures into Egyptology, so commentaries may be useful but Egypt-related claims should be treated cautiously. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=11162" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:02</a> | Should the church address racial inequality and climate change to revive? | He says creation care and redressing social ills can matter, but the church's central focus must remain the gospel and Christ's kingdom. | He affirms creation care as part of Christian practice and says social ills can matter, but warns against making climate change or political issues the church's central priority. He says the world is not the final destination and the church should focus on gospel and kingdom mission. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=11525" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:05</a> | What book, besides the Bible, changed his life? | He recalls a Kierkegaard book about the problem of being Christian as helpful, though he cannot remember the exact title. | He says a Kierkegaard book, which he remembers as something like The Problem of Being Christian, helped him during a low point. He valued it because Kierkegaard was a Christian who struggled with faith and named those struggles honestly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=11689" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:14:49</a> | Is Wisdom 2:12-20 prophecy, compared with Matthew 27? | He says he does not understand the proposed relation clearly enough and would need the question clarified. | He reads enough of the Wisdom passage to recognize the question is asking about an apocryphal text and Matthew 27, but says he does not see the relation being proposed. He gives an intentionally limited answer and asks for clarification rather than forcing an interpretation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=11844" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:17:24</a> | Were there Egyptian coastal forts east or northeast of Migdol on the Way of Horus, and did they have chariots? | He says yes, there were forts beyond Migdol, and the major forts on the route would have had chariots. | He says there were at least two forts east of Migdol on the Road of Horus, though exact identifications are limited because only some forts have been found. He says chariots were stationed at the major forts, with numbers varying by location. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=11991" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:51</a> | What are his thoughts on Dan McClellan's video on the no true Scotsman fallacy? | He says McClellan misunderstands the fallacy and treats adding words like true or genuine as sufficient when it is not. | He says he has strong criticism reserved for an upcoming video and thinks McClellan misunderstands the no true Scotsman fallacy. The criticism is that McClellan treats the mere use of qualifying language like true or genuine as though it automatically creates the fallacy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=12325" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:25:25</a> | What books are best for learning about ancient Egypt's relationship to and influence on the Bible? | He recommends James Hoffmeier's work, especially books on Israel in Egypt and Israel in Sinai. | He recommends James Hoffmeier as a solid Egyptologist who has published extensively on Egypt and the Bible. He singles out Israel in Egypt and Israel in Sinai, noting that the Sinai volume is more technical but useful for the Egyptian influence question. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=12539" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:59</a> | How does he respond to Dan McClellan's claim that Jesus was not God but a divine name bearer? | He rejects it as a critical-scholarship reading that pits texts against each other and does not persuade him. | He says he has already criticized claims that the Gospels do not present Jesus as God. He sees the divine-name-bearer claim as part of a critical method that isolates texts against one another and reduces Jesus to an exalted man rather than accepting the broader New Testament witness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=12684" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:31:24</a> | When is he going to play the guitar on stream? | He says never, because he is not a performer. | He gives a direct refusal: he is not a performer and does not intend to play guitar on stream. He also rejects the idea that a price tag would change that. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=12767" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:47</a> | How would a pharaoh who did not go to war with his troops have been viewed, and would building projects compensate? | He says not every pharaoh campaigned, but kings still had to ensure safety, tribute, food supply, and order. | He says lack of campaigning did not automatically make a pharaoh look weak, since some kings maintained authority without major wars. Building projects could compensate in some reigns, but the core royal duty was still keeping the country safe, supplied, orderly, and receiving tribute. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=12976" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:36:16</a> | What is the progress on his book about the ten plagues? | He says he is working on the proposal, now has needed publisher information, and hopes to finish it in a few weeks. | He says he has not yet moved into full writing because the proposal has gone slowly while waiting on publisher information. Now that he has that information and is full-time, the proposal has become a priority and he hopes to finish it soon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=13131" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:38:51</a> | How does he feel about modern Gospel adaptations in media like The Chosen? | He says adaptations can be useful, but reading the Gospels is better and modern sensibilities can intrude when creators fill gaps. | He takes a middle position: Gospel adaptations can help people encounter the story, but reading the Gospels is better. The danger is that when creators fill narrative gaps, modern assumptions or sensibilities can be inserted into the biblical material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=13712" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:48:32</a> | Is a uniliteral Egyptian hieroglyphic text possible for biblical material? | He says he does not see why it would be needed when early alphabetic inscription systems already existed. | He says he does not see the need for writing biblical material with a uniliteral Egyptian hieroglyphic system. If early alphabetic inscriptional character sets were already available, they would be the more natural tool than adapting Egyptian uniliterals. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=13922" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:02</a> | What was the purpose of uniting Amun and Re? | He says it consolidated sun-cult and royal-cult functions around the Theban dynasty and Karnak. | He explains that Amun and Re were both treated as kingly deities: Amun as the hidden royal power of Thebes and Re as the solar royal god. Their union consolidated the sun cult with the royal cult of the Theban dynasty as Karnak developed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/qQuVwgNpPk8?t=14025" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:53:45</a> | How and when was the Egyptian Book of Dreams codified? | He says the evidence is limited to Chester Beatty III, a 19th Dynasty copy of a probably earlier text, so the exact codification history is unknown. | He says the surviving evidence is essentially Chester Beatty III, a Deir el-Medina manuscript from Dynasty 19, with a letter from a vizier on the back. Because the text is already well developed there, he thinks it is probably copied from earlier tradition, but the codification date is unknown. |
